title.emphasis: Section III: Analysis of Federal and Military Jurisdiction

section.para: Operating in parallel to state laws are distinct federal and military legal frameworks that govern sexual offenses within their specific jurisdictional boundaries.

title.emphasis: 3.1 Federal Law: Scope and Limitations (18 U.S. Code Chapter 109A)

section.para: Federal jurisdiction over sexual offenses is limited and typically applies to crimes that occur on federal lands (e.g., national parks, military bases), involve interstate commerce, or are committed against federal employees.85 Federal law, under 18 U.S.C. § 2242, defines sexual abuse as engaging in a sexual act with another person who is incapable of appraising the nature of the conduct, is physically incapable of declining participation, or does so without that person's consent.86 For offenses that cross state lines or involve online communication, federal law generally sets the age of consent at 18.1

title.emphasis: 3.2 Military Law: The Uniform Code of Military Justice (UCMJ)

section.para: The United States military is governed by its own legal code, the Uniform Code of Military Justice (UCMJ). Article 120 of the UCMJ defines the offenses of rape, sexual assault, aggravated sexual contact, and abusive sexual contact.87 The UCMJ operates on a non-consensual framework, criminalizing sexual acts committed by force, threat, or when the victim is unconscious, incapacitated, or otherwise unable to consent.91 The military justice system is a distinct legal entity with its own prosecutorial structure, which now includes the Offices of Special Trial Counsel (OSTC) to independently prosecute these offenses.87 The act of enlistment functions as a form of implied consent to the military's hierarchical command structure, but this consent does not extend to sexual conduct, which is strictly governed by the definitions in Article 120.1
